title: On the viability of Transatlantic Quantum Entanglement Distribution using Combined Satellite and Stratospheric Relay Nodes
authors: Kimia Mohammadi, Paul J. Godin, Thomas Jennewein
abstract: To explore the pathways toward establishing a global quantum network, we investigate several link architectures for transatlantic quantum entanglement distribution over a 6,500 km ground distance. We define free-space link configurations involving satellites and stratospheric high altitude platforms (HAPs), using today's technology and without relying on quantum memories and repeaters. Considering link budgets, space radiation, orbital characteristics, and system complexity we find that a hybrid architecture consisting of an entangled photon source located on a low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite supported by two passive optical relays located on HAPs provides the overall highest entanglement distribution rate. In addition, the satellite HAP architecture offers practical advantages in payload design and launch requirements, and the ability to lower the weather-related link interruptions assuming some maneuverability of HAPs. Overall, this hybrid configuration yields on the order of 5X10^6 secure key bits per year using 30 cm aperture ground receivers, nearly two orders of magnitude higher than achievable with a single MEO satellite and 1 m aperture ground receivers. Our results highlight the major benefits of hybrid satellite HAP architectures by reducing system complexity while enabling scalable and more accessible long-range quantum communication networks.
summary: This paper analyzes methods for achieving transatlantic quantum entanglement distribution using satellites and stratospheric relays. By modeling various link architectures, the authors determined that a hybrid LEO satellite and HAP system provides the highest secure key rate ($\approx 5 	imes 10^6$ bits/year). This demonstrates the significant advantage of incorporating low-cost, maneuverable atmospheric relays over single high-altitude satellites.
main_problem: The study investigates feasible architectures for distributing quantum entanglement across transatlantic distances (6,500 km) to establish a global quantum network.
main_result: A hybrid architecture combining an entangled photon source on a Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite with two passive High-Altitude Platform (HAP) relays yields the highest entanglement distribution rate ($\approx 5 	imes 10^6$ secure key bits/year).
method: The authors modeled various free-space link configurations, comparing single MEO satellites against hybrid LEO+HAP systems by analyzing link budgets and achievable key rates.
model_or_system: The system involves ground stations communicating with space assets (LEO/MEO satellites) and stratospheric HAPs acting as passive optical relays. The analysis incorporates orbital mechanics, free-space link loss calculations, and quantum key distribution metrics.
key_observables: Entanglement distribution rate (secure key bits per year), Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER), and violation of the CHSH Bell inequality parameter ($S$).
important_parameters: Transatlantic distance ($\sim 6,500$ km); LEO altitude ($\sim 1,200$ km); HAP altitude ($\sim 20$ km); Ground receiver aperture sizes (e.g., $30 	ext{ cm}$ vs $1 	ext{ m}$).
main_assumptions: The study assumes that HAPs act purely as passive relays and does not rely on quantum memories or repeaters for entanglement distribution.
figures_summary: Figures compare link loss and elevation angle variations across different satellite constellations (MEO vs. LEO+HAP); Figure 7 illustrates the relationship between Bell-violation parameters and QBER requirements.
paper_structure: The paper systematically compares multiple link architectures (single MEO, multi-LEO, LEO+HAP) by calculating key performance indicators like achievable secure key rates and verifying entanglement preservation via Bell tests.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the engineering challenges of scaling quantum communication networks over continental distances by proposing a practical hybrid space/air relay solution that significantly outperforms purely satellite-based approaches.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
